The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=35).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 17 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Square 2 moved from (36-37, 40-41) to (30-31, 34-35) in ONE click! I clicked (31, 35) and the square moved so that (31, 35) became its bottom-right corner. The distance from the original square (36-37, 40-41) to the clicked cell (31, 35) was about 5-6 rows and 5-6 cols. So the range is MUCH larger than I thought - it seems unlimited!
This means I can move ANY square to ANY position in a single click! The mechanic is: clicking a cell moves the NEAREST square so that the clicked cell becomes its bottom-right corner.
Now the plan is simple: click on 4 positions within the blue blob, one for each square. Each click will move the nearest square to that position.
Current M squares:
1. (26-27, 9-10) - needs to move right to blob
2. (30-31, 34-35) - already on the blob!
3. (53-54, 48-49) - needs to move up-left to blob
4. (54-55, 13-14) - needs to move up-right to blob
The blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37. I need to move squares 1, 3, and 4 to positions within the blob.
For square 1 at (26-27, 9-10), I can click at (27, 33) to move it to (26-27, 32-33).
For square 3 at (53-54, 48-49), I can click at (29, 35) to move it to (28-29, 34-35).
For square 4 at (54-55, 13-14), I can click at (30, 31) to move it to (29-30, 30-31).
But wait, I need to be careful about which square is "nearest" to each click. Let me plan the clicks so that the correct square is nearest to each click position.
Actually, since I can move squares to any position, I should click positions that are:
1. Within the blob
2. Closest to the square I want to move
Let me plan:
- Move square 1 (26-27, 9-10): click at (27, 29) → square moves to (26-27, 28-29)
  - Distance to square 1: ~20 cols
  - Distance to square 3: ~27 rows + ~20 cols
  - Distance to square 4: ~28 rows + ~14 cols
  - Square 1 is nearest ✓
- Move square 3 (53-54, 48-49): click at (29, 37) → square moves to (28-29, 36-37)
  - Distance to square 3: ~24 rows + ~12 cols
  - Distance to square 4: ~26 rows + ~12 cols
  - Square 3 is nearest ✓
- Move square 4 (54-55, 13-14): click at (31, 31) → square moves to (30-31, 30-31)
  - Distance to square 4: ~24 rows + ~18 cols
  - Distance to square 3: ~23 rows + ~18 cols
  - Hmm, square 3 might be closer... Let me recalculate.
Actually, after moving square 3, it will be at (28-29, 36-37), which is much closer to the blob center. So I should move square 4 first, then square 3.
Let me reorder:
1. Move square 4 (54-55, 13-14): click at (31, 31) → square moves to (30-31, 30-31)
   - Distance to square 4: ~25 rows + ~18 cols = ~43
   - Distance to square 3: ~24 rows + ~18 cols = ~42
   - These are very close! Square 3 might be nearer...
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me think about it differently. The "nearest" square is determined by some distance metric. If I click at (31, 31):
- Distance to square 4 center (54.5, 13.5): sqrt(23.5² + 17.5²) ≈ 29
- Distance to square 3 center (53.5, 48.5): sqrt(22.5² + 17.5²) ≈ 28.5
Square 3 is slightly closer! So clicking (31, 31) would move square 3, not square 4.
To move square 4, I need to click closer to it. Let me click at (31, 25):
- Distance to square 4 center (54.5, 13.5): sqrt(23.5² + 11.5²) ≈ 25.9
- Distance to square 3 center (53.5, 48.5): sqrt(22.5² + 23.5²) ≈ 32.4
Square 4 is closer! Good.
So the plan:
1. Click (31, 25) → moves square 4 to (30-31, 24-25)
2. Click (29, 37) → moves square 3 to (28-29, 36-37)
3. Click (27, 33) → moves square 1 to (26-27, 32-33)
But wait, after step 1, square 4 is at (30-31, 24-25). Then in step 2, clicking (29, 37):
- Distance to square 4 (30.5, 24.5): sqrt(1.5² + 12.5²) ≈ 12.6
- Distance to square 3 (53.5, 48.5): sqrt(24.5² + 11.5²) ≈ 27
Square 4 is closer! So clicking (29, 37) would move square 4 again, not square 3.
I need to be more careful. After moving square 4, it's close to the blob. Then clicking near the blob would move square 4 again.
Maybe I should move the farthest squares first, and move them to positions within the blob. Then the closer squares can be moved last.
Let me think about this more carefully. The key constraint is: after moving a square to the blob, subsequent clicks near the blob will move that square again (since it's now the nearest).
So the strategy should be:
1. Move the farthest square to the blob first.
2. Then move the next farthest square to a different part of the blob.
3. Continue until all squares are on the blob.
But the problem is that once a square is on the blob, clicking elsewhere to move another square might accidentally move the already-positioned square.
Actually, let me reconsider. If I click far from the blob, the nearest square will be one of the far squares, not the one on the blob. So I can still move the far squares even after positioning one on the blob.
Let me plan more carefully:
Current squares:
1. (26-27, 9-10)
2. (30-31, 34-35) - already on blob
3. (53-54, 48-49)
4. (54-55, 13-14)
Step 1: Move square 3 (farthest, at 53-54, 48-49) to blob
- Click at (29, 37) - this is within the blob
- Distance to square 3: ~24 rows + 12 cols
- Distance to square 4: ~25 rows + 18 cols
- Square 3 is closer ✓
- Square 3 moves to (28-29, 36-37)
Step 2: Move square 4 (54-55, 13-14) to blob
- Click at (31, 31) - this is within the blob
- Distance to square 4: ~25 rows + 18 cols
- Distance to square 3 (now at 28-29, 36-37): ~3 rows + 6 cols
- Square 3 is MUCH closer! This would move square 3 again, not square 4.
So I need to click far from the blob to target square 4. Let me click at (40, 20):
- Distance to square 4 (54.5, 13.5): ~14.5 rows + 6.5 cols ≈ 16
- Distance to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~11.5 rows + 16.5 cols ≈ 20
- Square 4 is closer ✓
- Square 4 moves to (39-40, 19-20)
Then I need another click to move square 4 from (39-40, 19-20) to the blob:
- Click at (31, 29):
  - Distance to square 4 (39.5, 19.5): ~8.5 rows + 9.5 cols ≈ 12.7
  - Distance to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 rows + 7.5 cols ≈ 7.9
  - Square 3 is closer! This would move square 3 again.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. The issue is that once squares are on the blob, they become the nearest to any blob-adjacent click.
Maybe the solution is to move all squares to the blob in a specific order, clicking positions that are closest to the target square but far from already-positioned squares.
Actually, let me try a different approach. What if I click positions that are BETWEEN the far squares and the blob? This way, the far square is still the nearest.
For square 4 at (54-55, 13-14), I want to move it to the blob. Let me click at (45, 25):
- Distance to square 4 (54.5, 13.5): ~9.5 rows + 11.5 cols ≈ 15
- Distance to square 1 (26.5, 9.5): ~18.5 rows + 15.5 cols ≈ 24.2
- Distance to square 3 (53.5, 48.5): ~8.5 rows + 23.5 cols ≈ 25.1
- Distance to square 2 (30.5, 34.5): ~14.5 rows + 9.5 cols ≈ 17.4
Square 4 is closest! ✓
Square 4 moves to (44-45, 24-25).
Then click at (35, 30):
- Distance to square 4 (44.5, 24.5): ~9.5 rows + 5.5 cols ≈ 11
- Distance to square 1 (26.5, 9.5): ~8.5 rows + 20.5 cols ≈ 22.2
- Distance to square 3 (53.5, 48.5): ~18.5 rows + 18.5 cols ≈ 26
- Distance to square 2 (30.5, 34.5): ~4.5 rows + 4.5 cols ≈ 6.4
Square 2 is closest! This would move square 2, not square 4.
This is really tricky. Let me try yet another approach: move each square directly to the blob in one click, by clicking at a position within the blob that's closest to that square.
For square 3 (53-54, 48-49), click at (29, 37) - closest blob point to square 3.
For square 4 (54-55, 13-14), click at (31, 29) - closest blob point to square 4.
For square 1 (26-27, 9-10), click at (27, 29) - closest blob point to square 1.
But the problem is that after moving square 3 to (28-29, 36-37), clicking (31, 29) would be closer to square 3 than to square 4.
OK, I think the solution is to move squares in order of distance from the blob, starting with the CLOSEST square first. That way, after moving the closest square to the blob, the next click (which targets a farther square) will still be closer to the farther square than to the already-moved square.
Wait, that's backwards. Let me think again...
If I move the closest square first, it ends up on the blob. Then when I try to move a farther square by clicking on the blob, the already-moved square (now on the blob) is closer to the click than the farther square. So the click moves the wrong square.
If I move the farthest square first, it ends up on the blob. Then when I try to move a closer square by clicking on the blob, the already-moved square (on the blob) might be closer to the click than the closer square. Hmm, same problem.
Actually, the key insight is: I should click positions that are NOT on the blob, but rather positions that are on the path between the target square and the blob. This way, the target square is still the nearest.
Let me try a step-by-step approach for each square:
1. Move square 3 (53-54, 48-49) toward the blob:
   - Click at (45, 40) - halfway between square 3 and blob
   - Square 3 moves to (44-45, 39-40)
   - Click at (35, 37) - closer to blob
   - Square 3 moves to (34-35, 36-37)
   - Click at (29, 37) - on blob
   - Square 3 moves to (28-29, 36-37)
That's 3 clicks for square 3.
2. Move square 4 (54-55, 13-14) toward the blob:
   - Click at (45, 20) - halfway
   - Square 4 moves to (44-45, 19-20)
   - Click at (35, 25) - closer
   - Square 4 moves to (34-35, 24-25)
   - Click at (31, 31) - on blob
   - But square 3 is now at (28-29, 36-37), which is closer to (31, 31) than square 4 at (34-35, 24-25)!
   - Distance from (31, 31) to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 + 5.5 = 8
   - Distance from (31, 31) to square 4 (34.5, 24.5): ~3.5 + 6.5 = 10
   - Square 3 is closer! Wrong square would move.
   - Instead, click at (31, 27):
     - Distance to square 4 (34.5, 24.5): ~3.5 + 2.5 = 6
     - Distance to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 + 9.5 = 12
     - Square 4 is closer! ✓
   - Square 4 moves to (30-31, 26-27)
   - Click at (31, 31):
     - Distance to square 4 (30.5, 26.5): ~0.5 + 4.5 = 5
     - Distance to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 + 5.5 = 8
     - Square 4 is closer! ✓
   - Square 4 moves to (30-31, 30-31)
That's 5 clicks for square 4.
This is getting very expensive. Let me think of a better strategy.
Actually, maybe the goal isn't to move ALL squares to the blob. Maybe only some squares need to move. Or maybe the squares change color when they touch the blob, and then they need to move to the yellow square.
Let me just try moving one square to the blob and see what happens. If it changes color or triggers something, I'll know the goal.
Actually, square 2 is already at (30-31, 34-35), which overlaps with the blob (rows 23-31, cols 29-37). Let me check if it changed color...
Looking at the output, square 2 is still M (magenta), not p (purple). So being on the blob doesn't automatically change its color.
Hmm, maybe I need to do something else. Maybe ACTION7 is the "confirm" or "apply" action that processes the squares on the blob.
Let me try ACTION7 now that square 2 is on the blob.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Square 2 is already on the blob at (30-31, 34-35)
# Let me try ACTION7 to see if it processes squares on the blob
result = action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"ACTION7 result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nM squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  M: {n['boundary']}")
print("\np squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  p: {n['boundary']}")
print("\nY squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  Y: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Distance to square 3 (53.5, 48.5): ~18.5 rows + 18.5 cols ≈ 26
- Distance to square 2 (30.5, 34.5): ~4.5 rows + 4.5 cols ≈ 6.4
Square 2 is closest! This would move square 2, not square 4.
This is really tricky. Let me try yet another approach: move each square directly to the blob in one click, by clicking at a position within the blob that's closest to that square.
For square 3 (53-54, 48-49), click at (29, 37) - closest blob point to square 3.
For square 4 (54-55, 13-14), click at (31, 29) - closest blob point to square 4.
For square 1 (26-27, 9-10), click at (27, 29) - closest blob point to square 1.
But the problem is that after moving square 3 to (28-29, 36-37), clicking (31, 29) would be closer to square 3 than to square 4.
OK, I think the solution is to move squares in order of distance from the blob, starting with the CLOSEST square first. That way, after moving the closest square to the blob, the next click (which targets a farther square) will still be closer to the farther square than to the already-moved square.
Wait, that's backwards. Let me think again...
If I move the closest square first, it ends up on the blob. Then when I try to move a farther square by clicking on the blob, the already-moved square (now on the blob) is closer to the click than the farther square. So the click moves the wrong square.
If I move the farthest square first, it ends up on the blob. Then when I try to move a closer square by clicking on the blob, the already-moved square (on the blob) might be closer to the click than the closer square. Hmm, same problem.
Actually, the key insight is: I should click positions that are NOT on the blob, but rather positions that are on the path between the target square and the blob. This way, the target square is still the nearest.
Let me try a step-by-step approach for each square:
1. Move square 3 (53-54, 48-49) toward the blob:
   - Click at (45, 40) - halfway between square 3 and blob
   - Square 3 moves to (44-45, 39-40)
   - Click at (35, 37) - closer to blob
   - Square 3 moves to (34-35, 36-37)
   - Click at (29, 37) - on blob
   - Square 3 moves to (28-29, 36-37)
That's 3 clicks for square 3.
2. Move square 4 (54-55, 13-14) toward the blob:
   - Click at (45, 20) - halfway
   - Square 4 moves to (44-45, 19-20)
   - Click at (35, 25) - closer
   - Square 4 moves to (34-35, 24-25)
   - Click at (31, 31) - on blob
   - But square 3 is now at (28-29, 36-37), which is closer to (31, 31) than square 4 at (34-35, 24-25)!
   - Distance from (31, 31) to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 + 5.5 = 8
   - Distance from (31, 31) to square 4 (34.5, 24.5): ~3.5 + 6.5 = 10
   - Square 3 is closer! Wrong square would move.
   - Instead, click at (31, 27):
     - Distance to square 4 (34.5, 24.5): ~3.5 + 2.5 = 6
     - Distance to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 + 9.5 = 12
     - Square 4 is closer! ✓
   - Square 4 moves to (30-31, 26-27)
   - Click at (31, 31):
     - Distance to square 4 (30.5, 26.5): ~0.5 + 4.5 = 5
     - Distance to square 3 (28.5, 36.5): ~2.5 + 5.5 = 8
     - Square 4 is closer! ✓
   - Square 4 moves to (30-31, 30-31)
That's 5 clicks for square 4.
This is getting very expensive. Let me think of a better strategy.
Actually, maybe the goal isn't to move ALL squares to the blob. Maybe only some squares need to move. Or maybe the squares change color when they touch the blob, and then they need to move to the yellow square.
Let me just try moving one square to the blob and see what happens. If it changes color or triggers something, I'll know the goal.
Actually, square 2 is already at (30-31, 34-35), which overlaps with the blob (rows 23-31, cols 29-37). Let me check if it changed color...
Looking at the output, square 2 is still M (magenta), not p (purple). So being on the blob doesn't automatically change its color.
Hmm, maybe I need to do something else. Maybe ACTION7 is the "confirm" or "apply" action that processes the squares on the blob.
Let me try ACTION7 now that square 2 is on the blob.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Square 2 is already on the blob at (30-31, 34-35)
# Let me try ACTION7 to see if it processes squares on the blob
result = action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"ACTION7 result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nM squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  M: {n['boundary']}")
print("\np squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  p: {n['boundary']}")
print("\nY squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  Y: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
